question: Q4: Give one example each for the following with reference to secondary brand associations - a) Awareness and knowledge of the entity b) Cognitive consistency

model_answer.answer_text:
**Secondary Brand Associations** (Keller & Swaminathan, Ch.8, pp.292-295)

Keller defines secondary brand associations as associations that are not directly related to the product but are linked to other entities — such as people, places, things, or other brands — enabling a transfer of knowledge from that entity to the focal brand. Three conditions govern this transfer: (1) awareness and knowledge of the entity, (2) meaningfulness of the entity’s knowledge, and (3) transferability of the entity’s knowledge. Below, we illustrate two of these conditions with fresh Indian-market examples.

---

**a) Awareness and Knowledge of the Entity — VAHDAM India x Oprah Winfrey’s “Favorite Things” List**

Keller (Ch.8, p.294) states: “If consumers have no awareness or knowledge of the secondary entity, then obviously there is nothing they can transfer from it. Ideally, consumers would be aware of the entity; hold some strong, favorable, and perhaps even unique associations about it; and have positive judgments and feelings about it.”

VAHDAM India, a D2C premium tea brand founded by Bala Sarda in 2015, exemplifies this condition powerfully. In November 2018, VAHDAM became the first Indian company ever to be featured in Oprah Winfrey’s prestigious “Favorite Things” annual list — and was featured again in 2019, the only Indian brand to appear two years running [1]. Oprah declared in O, The Oprah Magazine: “I’m in love with a wonderful chai! Or three, to be exact — Spiced Oolong, Ginger and Sweet Cinnamon, shipped directly from India” [2].

Here, the secondary entity is Oprah Winfrey herself. Oprah commands enormous global awareness — she is widely recognized as a cultural tastemaker, a trustworthy voice, and a connoisseur of quality products. Consumers already held strong, favorable, and unique associations about her: authenticity, discerning taste, and credibility. When Oprah linked herself to VAHDAM by including it in her curated list, consumers could immediately transfer these associations onto an otherwise unknown Indian tea startup. As founder Bala Sarda noted in an interview: “Oprah Winfrey drinking and endorsing our teas was a great validation for the brand… it told us that a good product finds its audience” [3]. The brand subsequently grew to serve over 4 million customers across 130+ countries and entered 4,000+ stores in CVS and Costco in the US [4]. This illustrates Keller’s point precisely — without Oprah’s pre-existing awareness and favorable knowledge among global consumers, there would have been nothing to transfer, and VAHDAM would have remained just another Indian tea brand competing for shelf space.

---

**b) Cognitive Consistency — Plum Goodness x PETA Certification**

Keller (Ch.8, p.294) explains that for secondary associations to transfer, cognitive consistency must operate: “In the minds of consumers, what is true for the entity must be true for the brand.”

Plum Goodness, founded by Shankar Prasad (ex-HUL) in 2014, became India’s first beauty brand to be 100% vegan and PETA-certified from its very inception [5]. PETA (People for the Ethical Treatment of Animals) is the world’s largest animal rights organization, and its certification is globally recognized as the gold standard for cruelty-free and ethical product practices. The entity here is PETA as a third-party source (one of Keller’s eight types of secondary entities).

Cognitive consistency operates as follows: consumers already associate PETA with uncompromising animal welfare, zero tolerance for animal testing, and rigorous ethical standards. When Plum displays the PETA-certified logo on every product, cognitive consistency dictates that what is true for PETA must be true for Plum — if PETA certifies this brand, then this brand MUST be genuinely cruelty-free, ethical, and trustworthy. Consumers do not need independent verification; the PETA certification alone triggers the inferential leap. As one industry analysis noted: “Research from 2016 to 2023 has consistently played back that consumers think of Plum as a brand with its heart in the right place” [6]. Prasad himself was motivated by this mechanism — he found that Indian shelves lacked PETA-certified options, and he knew that securing this certification would serve as a powerful trust signal [7].

The cognitive consistency transfer has been commercially validated: Plum grew to INR 400+ crore in revenue, expanded to 13,000+ retail outlets across 300+ cities, and attracted investment from Unilever Ventures, A91 Partners, and Faering Capital [5]. The PETA certification did not just add a logo — it transferred the entire ethical credibility architecture of PETA onto a then-unknown Indian D2C skincare brand, precisely through the cognitive consistency mechanism Keller describes.

---

**References:**

[1] “Vahdam Teas First Indian Company to Make It to Oprah’s List,” Destination Reporter India, 23 Nov 2018. URL: https://destinationreporterindia.com/2018/11/23/vahdam-teas-first-indian-company-to-make-it-to-oprahs-list/

[2] “Oprah’s Favorite Things 2018 Full List — Chai Teas Private Reserve Trio,” O, The Oprah Magazine / Oprah.com, 2018. URL: https://www.oprah.com/gift/oprahs-favorite-things-2018-full-list-chai-teas-private-reserve-trio_1

[3] A. Jain, “Interview: Vahdam’s Bala Sarda on creating global footprint for a brand endorsed by Oprah Winfrey, Ellen DeGeneres,” Social Samosa, Dec 2020. URL: https://www.socialsamosa.com/2020/12/interview-vahdam-marketing-strategy-bala-sarda/

[4] “How VAHDAM India is Positioning Indian-Origin Tea on the Global Stage,” Indian Retailer, 2023. URL: https://www.indianretailer.com/article/retail-business/marvels/how-vahdam-india-positioning-indian-origin-tea-global-stage

[5] “Plum Goodness: The Indian Cruelty-Free Beauty Brand Breaking Barriers in 2025,” Brands Pe Charcha, 11 Apr 2025. URL: https://brandspecharcha.com/2025/04/11/plum-goodness-the-indian-cruelty-free-beauty-brand-breaking-barriers-in-2025/

[6] “Why Good Equals Great for Plum,” The Hard Copy, 2023. URL: https://thehardcopy.co/why-good-equals-great-for-plum/

[7] “Sustainable Beauty — Plum,” AIM2Flourish. URL: https://aim2flourish.com/innovations/sustainable-beauty

brand_bank.entity_type: Celebrity/Influencer (Oprah Winfrey) + Third-party list (Oprah’s Favorite Things)
brand_bank.sub_part: a) Awareness and knowledge of the entity
brand_bank.brand_concept: Oprah is one of the most recognized and trusted media personalities globally. Her pre-existing awareness and strong favorable associations (taste-maker, authentic, discerning) transferred directly to an unknown Indian tea brand when she featured it in her Favorite Things list in 2018 and 2019. Without Oprah’s massive pre-existing consumer awareness, there would be nothing to transfer — illustrating Keller’s first condition perfectly.
brand_bank.how_to_use: First Indian company on Oprah’s Favorite Things list (2018). Grew to 4M+ customers in 130+ countries. Featured 2 consecutive years.

citation.author: Indian Retailer
citation.title: How VAHDAM India is Positioning Indian-Origin Tea on the Global Stage
citation.quoted_text: VAHDAM is the only Indian brand available in over 7000 stores in the US, 250 stores in Canada, 550 stores in the UK.

brand_bank.brand_concept: PETA is globally synonymous with animal rights and cruelty-free ethics. When Plum became PETA-certified, cognitive consistency operates: what is true for PETA (zero animal cruelty, ethical standards) MUST be true for Plum in consumers’ minds. Consumers infer without independent verification that Plum is genuinely ethical — the PETA seal triggers the inferential transfer.
brand_bank.how_to_use: India’s first 100% vegan and PETA-certified beauty brand from inception (2014). INR 400+ Cr revenue. 13,000+ retail outlets.

citation.author: Brands Pe Charcha
citation.title: Plum Goodness: The Indian Cruelty-Free Beauty Brand Breaking Barriers in 2025
citation.quoted_text: Plum is India’s first 100% vegan beauty and PETA cruelty-free brand… Backed by investors like A91 Partners, Unilever Ventures, and Faering Capital.

brand_bank.brand_concept: Kohli is associated with extreme fitness discipline and performance tracking. When he endorses Noise smartwatches (fitness trackers), cognitive consistency means consumers infer: what is true for Kohli (fitness-obsessed, data-driven athlete) must be true for Noise (accurate fitness tracking, performance-oriented). The #SunoDilKaShor campaign reinforced this overlap.
brand_bank.how_to_use: India’s No.1 smartwatch brand for 9 consecutive quarters. Kohli appointed brand ambassador Dec 2022.

citation.title: Noise (smartwatches) analysis
citation.quoted_text: Kohli is associated with extreme fitness discipline and performance tracking. When he endorses Noise

brand_bank.brand_concept: Shilpa Shetty is widely known for wellness, yoga, and healthy living. Mamaearth leveraged her pre-existing awareness and strong favorable associations with natural, toxin-free living to build trust for their natural personal care products. Consumers already knew Shilpa and held positive health/wellness associations.
brand_bank.how_to_use: Shilpa Shetty is both investor and brand ambassador. Mamaearth’s parent Honasa Consumer went public (IPO 2023).

citation.title: Mamaearth analysis
